annotations.label: Straightedge-and-compass construction

anchor.snippet: straightedge-and-compass construction – also known as ruler-and-compass construction

annotations.note: No definition of straightedge-and-compass constructions exists in Mathlib; the only `Constructible` namespaces refer to constructible topology/sets in algebraic geometry.

annotations.label: Idealized straightedge and compass

anchor.snippet: The idealized ruler, known as a straightedge , is assumed to be infinite in length

annotations.note: Mathlib has no formalization of the idealized straightedge or compass tools.

annotations.label: Compass-only and straightedge-only constructibility

anchor.snippet: every point constructible using straightedge and compass may also be constructed using compass alone

annotations.note: No formalization of Mohr–Mascheroni or related compass/straightedge-only equivalences in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Wantzel's impossibility results

anchor.snippet: Some of the most famous straightedge-and-compass problems were proved impossible by Pierre Wantzel

annotations.note: Wantzel's impossibility theorems are not in Mathlib (no Wantzel-named declarations found).

annotations.label: Algebraic characterization of constructible lengths and angles

anchor.snippet: a length is constructible if and only if it represents a constructible number

annotations.note: Mathlib does not define constructible lengths or constructible numbers.

annotations.label: Characterization of constructible numbers

anchor.snippet: A number is constructible if and only if it can be written using the four basic arithmetic operations

annotations.note: No notion of `ConstructibleNumber` or square-root tower closure exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Straightedge

anchor.snippet: The straightedge is an infinitely long edge with no markings on it.

annotations.note: No formalization of an idealized straightedge in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compass

anchor.snippet: The compass can have an arbitrarily large radius with no markings on it

annotations.note: No formalization of an idealized compass in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Compass equivalence theorem

anchor.snippet: by the compass equivalence theorem in Proposition 2 of Book 1 of Euclid's Elements , no power is lost by using a collapsing compass

annotations.note: Compass equivalence (Euclid I.2) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Exactness and termination of constructions

anchor.snippet: Each construction must be mathematically exact

annotations.note: No formal definition of valid finite-step constructions in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Greek constructions of sums, products, and square roots

anchor.snippet: they discovered how to construct sums , differences , products , ratios , and square roots of given lengths

annotations.note: No formalization of constructibility of these arithmetic operations geometrically in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Hippocrates and Menaechmus doubling the cube

anchor.snippet: Hippocrates and Menaechmus showed that the volume of the cube could be doubled by finding the intersections of hyperbolas and parabolas

annotations.note: Conic-intersection cube-doubling construction is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss's constructible 17-gon and sufficient criterion

anchor.snippet: in 1796 Gauss showed that a regular polygon with 17 sides could be constructed

annotations.note: Mathlib has Fermat numbers but no Gauss/17-gon constructibility result.

annotations.label: Wantzel's impossibility proof

anchor.snippet: In 1837 Pierre Wantzel published a proof of the impossibility of trisecting an arbitrary angle

annotations.note: Wantzel's impossibility theorem (trisection / cube duplication) is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Lindemann's transcendence of pi

anchor.snippet: Then in 1882 Lindemann showed that

annotations.note: Only the analytical part of Lindemann's theorem is formalized; transcendence of π itself is not yet in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Five basic constructions

anchor.snippet: All straightedge-and-compass constructions consist of repeated application of five basic constructions

annotations.note: Mathlib does not enumerate or formalize the five basic Euclidean construction steps.

annotations.label: Equilateral triangle from two points

anchor.snippet: starting with just two distinct points, we can create a line or either of two circles

annotations.note: Euclid I.1 (equilateral triangle construction) is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Common straightedge-and-compass constructions

anchor.snippet: The most-used straightedge-and-compass constructions include

annotations.note: No catalog of standard constructions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Intersection points lie in a quadratic extension

anchor.snippet: the points at which they intersect lie in a quadratic extension of the smallest field

annotations.note: No theorem about line/circle intersection points living in a quadratic extension is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degree of minimal polynomial of constructible point is a power of 2

anchor.snippet: the degree of the minimal polynomial for a constructible point (and therefore of any constructible length) is a power of 2

annotations.note: Mathlib has general degree/tower lemmas but no constructibility ⇒ power-of-two-degree statement.

annotations.label: Bijection between constructible angles and points on a constructible circle

anchor.snippet: There is a bijection between the angles that are constructible and the points that are constructible on any constructible circle

annotations.note: No formalization of constructible angles in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Characterization of constructible angles by trig values

anchor.snippet: The angles that are constructible are exactly those whose tangent (or equivalently, sine or cosine) is constructible as a number

annotations.note: No constructible-angle/constructible-number link is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Finite-order constructible angles

anchor.snippet: The only angles of finite order that may be constructed starting with two points

annotations.note: Mathlib does not classify constructible rational multiples of π.

annotations.label: Constructible points as smallest field closed under conjugation and square roots

anchor.snippet: the points constructible using valid straightedge-and-compass constructions alone are precisely the elements of the smallest field

annotations.note: The smallest subfield of ℂ closed under conjugation and square roots is not formalized as constructible points in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Constructible ratios characterization

anchor.snippet: The set of ratios constructible using straightedge and compass from such a set of ratios is precisely the smallest field

annotations.note: Constructible-ratio characterization not present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Real part, imaginary part, modulus are constructible

anchor.snippet: the real part, imaginary part and modulus of a point or ratio

annotations.note: No statement about real/imaginary/modulus constructibility appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Cubic and transcendental obstructions

anchor.snippet: Doubling the cube and trisection of an angle

annotations.note: Impossibility via cubic minimal polynomial or transcendence is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: squaring the circle , otherwise known as the quadrature of the circle , involves constructing a square with the same area as a given circle

annotations.note: No formal definition of squaring the circle in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of squaring the circle

anchor.snippet: Squaring the circle has been proved impossible, as it involves generating a transcendental number

annotations.note: Impossibility of squaring the circle is unavailable since π's transcendence is not yet in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Kepler triangle approximation

anchor.snippet: A method that comes very close to approximating the "quadrature of the circle" can be achieved by using a Kepler triangle

annotations.note: No Kepler-triangle approximation result in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Doubling the cube

anchor.snippet: Doubling the cube is the construction, using only a straightedge and compass, of the edge of a cube that has twice the volume of another cube

annotations.note: Cube-doubling problem not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of doubling the cube

anchor.snippet: This is impossible because

annotations.note: Impossibility of cube-doubling not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angle trisection

anchor.snippet: Angle trisection is the construction, using only a straightedge and a compass, of an angle that is one-third of a given arbitrary angle

annotations.note: Angle trisection problem not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Impossibility of trisecting a 60° angle

anchor.snippet: a straightedge and compass construction cannot trisect a 60° angle

annotations.note: Impossibility of trisecting 60° not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Non-constructibility of distance to ellipse

anchor.snippet: the segment from any point in the plane to the nearest point on an ellipse of positive eccentricity cannot, in general, be constructed

annotations.note: No result on constructibility of the closest point on an ellipse in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neumann's theorem on Alhazen's problem

anchor.snippet: Peter M. Neumann proved the theorem that there is no ruler-and-compass construction for the general solution

annotations.note: Alhazen/Neumann result not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Gauss–Wantzel theorem on constructible regular polygons

anchor.snippet: a regular n -sided polygon can be constructed with straightedge and compass if the odd prime factors of n are distinct Fermat primes

annotations.note: Mathlib has `Nat.fermatNumber` but no Fermat-prime predicate nor a Gauss–Wantzel constructibility theorem.

annotations.label: Infinitely many even-sided constructible polygons

anchor.snippet: There are known to be an infinitude of constructible regular polygons with an even number of sides

annotations.note: No statement about infinitely many constructible regular polygons in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle construction from three of sixteen key points

anchor.section: Constructing a triangle from three given characteristic points or lengths
anchor.snippet: Sixteen key points of a triangle are its vertices

annotations.note: Catalog of triangle constructibility from key-point triples not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Triangle construction from key lengths and angles

anchor.section: Constructing a triangle from three given characteristic points or lengths
anchor.snippet: Twelve key lengths of a triangle are the three side lengths

annotations.note: Triangle constructibility from key length/angle triples is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Mohr–Mascheroni theorem

anchor.section: Constructing with only ruler or only compass
anchor.snippet: It is possible (according to the Mohr–Mascheroni theorem ) to construct anything with just a compass

annotations.note: Mohr–Mascheroni theorem is not in Mathlib (no such named declarations found).

annotations.label: Poncelet–Steiner theorem

anchor.section: Constructing with only ruler or only compass
anchor.snippet: Using just a ruler, it is impossible to take a square root, or mark the midpoint of a segment

annotations.note: Poncelet–Steiner theorem is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Planar, solid, and other constructions

anchor.snippet: The ancient Greeks classified constructions into three major categories

annotations.note: Greek classification of constructions not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Field-theoretic characterization of planar and solid numbers

anchor.snippet: a complex number that is planar has degree a power of two

annotations.note: No planar/solid number classification in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solid construction

anchor.snippet: A point has a solid construction if it can be constructed using a straightedge, compass, and a (possibly hypothetical) conic drawing tool

annotations.note: Solid (conic-assisted) construction definition is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Parabola y = x^2 enables solid constructions

anchor.snippet: using a compass, straightedge, and a piece of paper on which we have the parabola

annotations.note: Parabola-assisted constructions not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Solid construction of regular n-gon via Pierpont primes

anchor.snippet: A regular n -gon has a solid construction if and only if

annotations.note: No notion of Pierpont primes or solid-construction polygon characterization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Angle-trisection tool limits

anchor.snippet: Such constructions are solid constructions, but there exist numbers with solid constructions that cannot be constructed using such a tool

annotations.note: Limits of angle-trisection auxiliary tools not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Origami and Huzita–Hatori axioms

anchor.snippet: The mathematical theory of origami is more powerful than straightedge-and-compass construction

annotations.note: Huzita–Hatori axioms / origami constructibility not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Neusis construction

anchor.snippet: Archimedes , Nicomedes and Apollonius gave constructions involving the use of a markable ruler

annotations.note: Neusis (marked-ruler) constructions are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Power of the neusis construction

anchor.snippet: The neusis construction is more powerful than a conic drawing tool

annotations.note: Strength comparison of neusis vs conic constructions not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Plouffe's ruler-and-compass binary digit algorithm

anchor.snippet: In 1998 Simon Plouffe gave a ruler-and-compass algorithm that can be used to compute binary digits

annotations.note: Plouffe's binary-digit construction algorithm not formalized in Mathlib.
